lists.title: Portuguese A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 Brazilian Portuguese in communicative order: pronouns (você-based), noun gender, articles, ser/estar/ter, the three verb families, negation, tem/há, questions, and adjective agreement.

points.title: Subject pronouns (eu, você, ele…)
points.function: Say who you are talking about
points.explanation: eu (I), você (you — conjugated like he/she), ele/ela (he/she), nós (we), vocês (you all), eles/elas (they). Brazilian Portuguese uses você for 'you' in almost all situations; spoken Brazilian also loves a gente (literally 'the people') for 'we', conjugated in the singular.
points.culture_note: In Brazil, você is the everyday 'you'; tu survives in the South and parts of the Northeast (often with você's verb forms). In Portugal, tu is informal and você can sound distant — o senhor / a senhora is the polite form everywhere.

drills.translation: I am from Brazil.
I live in Rio de Janeiro.
Do you speak English?
Do you work here?
He is my brother.
He arrives tomorrow morning.
We are friends.
We live near the beach.
Do you (all) study Portuguese?
Do you (all) want a coffee?
They are from Bahia.
They play football on Sundays.

points.title: Gender and number of nouns
points.function: Use masculine and feminine, singular and plural
points.explanation:
Every noun is masculine or feminine: -o is usually masculine (o livro), -a usually feminine (a casa); -ão is often masculine, -ção feminine.

Plurals normally add -s (livro → livros); words in -ão vary (pão → pães, mão → mãos, coração → corações) and are learned per word.

drills.translation: The book is on the table.
The house is big and bright.
I bought two loaves at the bakery.
The girls arrive at eight.
The cars are in the parking lot.

drills.sentence: Ela lavou as {{answer}} antes do almoço.
drills.translation: She washed her hands before lunch.

points.title: Articles (o, a, um, uma)
points.function: Say 'the' and 'a' with the right gender
points.explanation:
Definite: o (m.), a (f.), os, as — Portuguese loves the article, even before names in Brazil (o João, a Maria).

Indefinite: um (m.), uma (f.), uns, umas for 'some'.

drills.translation: The sun is strong today.
The beach is near here.
I want a coffee, please.
She bought a new bicycle.
The boys are at school.
I invited some friends over for dinner.

points.title: Ser — present (sou, é, somos…)
points.function: Say who or what something IS (identity, origin)
points.explanation:
ser = permanent identity: profession, origin, character, time. eu sou, você/ele é, nós somos, vocês/eles são.

Sou professor 'I'm a teacher', ela é de São Paulo 'she's from São Paulo', são duas horas 'it's two o'clock'.

drills.translation: I am an engineer.
I am from Recife.
She is very funny.
Today is Friday.
We are neighbours.
We are from Minas Gerais.
They are doctors at the hospital.
The suitcases are heavy.

points.title: Estar — present (estou, está…)
points.function: Say how or where something is right now
points.explanation:
estar = temporary states and location: estou cansado 'I'm tired (now)', ela está em casa 'she's at home'. eu estou, você/ele está, nós estamos, vocês/eles estão.

Contrast with ser: ele é alegre (cheerful person) vs ele está alegre (happy right now).

drills.translation: I am hungry.
I am at work right now.
The coffee is hot.
She is at home today.
We are late!
We are at the bus stop.
The children are happy.
The documents are in the drawer.

points.title: Ter — present (tenho, tem…)
points.function: Say what you have — and how old you are
points.explanation:
ter = to have: eu tenho, você/ele tem, nós temos, vocês/eles têm.

Portuguese uses ter for age (tenho trinta anos 'I'm thirty') and for sensations (tenho sede 'I'm thirsty'). In spoken Brazil, tem also means 'there is' (see the Tem/Há point).

drills.translation: I have two sisters.
I am very thirsty. (lit. have thirst)
He has a blue car.
She is twenty-five years old.
We have a meeting at ten.
We have time for a coffee.
They have three children.
The houses here have backyards.

points.title: Present tense of -ar verbs
points.function: Talk about habits and current facts
points.explanation:
Drop -ar, add -o, -a, -amos, -am: falar → falo, fala, falamos, falam.

The present covers habits (trabalho de segunda a sexta 'I work Monday to Friday') and near-future plans with a time word (viajo amanhã).

drills.translation: I study Portuguese every day.
I work at a bank.
She speaks three languages.
He plays the guitar very well.
We dance samba at the party.
We visit our grandparents on Sundays.
They wake up early during the week.
They shop at the neighbourhood market.

points.title: -er and -ir verbs (comer, abrir)
points.function: Use the other two verb families
points.explanation:
-er verbs: como, come, comemos, comem (comer).

-ir verbs: abro, abre, abrimos, abrem (abrir) — identical to -er except nós -imos.

Common irregulars worth spotting early: fazer (faço), ir (vou), poder (posso), querer (quero).

drills.sentence: Eu {{answer}} pão com manteiga de manhã.
drills.translation: I eat bread and butter in the morning.
She reads a lot of books.
We drink orange juice.
The shop opens at nine.
I live in the city centre.

drills.sentence: Eles {{answer}} a conta no fim do mês.
drills.translation: They split the bill at the end of the month.

points.title: Negation with não
points.function: Say what is not so
points.explanation:
Put não before the verb: não falo japonês 'I don't speak Japanese'. Spoken Brazilian often doubles it for emphasis at the end: não sei não 'I really don't know'.

Answering a question, Portuguese repeats the verb: — Você fala inglês? — Falo. / Não falo.

drills.translation: I don't eat meat.
She doesn't live here anymore.
We don't have class today.
They don't like waking up early.
The bus doesn't run on Sundays.
Sorry, I didn't understand.

points.title: Tem / Há — there is, there are
points.function: Say what exists
points.explanation: In spoken Brazil, tem does the job of 'there is/are': tem um mercado aqui perto 'there's a market nearby'. há is the formal/written equivalent (há muitas opções) and also means 'ago' with time: há dois anos 'two years ago'. Both are invariable — never 'têm' for existence.

drills.translation: Is there a bank on this street?
There are a lot of people at the beach today.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} vagas para o curso da manhã.
drills.translation: There are places available in the morning course.
There's no problem at all.
I've lived here for two years. (lit. there are two years)
There's a great restaurant on the corner.

points.title: Question words (onde, o que, quando…)
points.function: Ask for information
points.explanation: onde (where), o que (what), quando (when), quem (who), como (how), por que (why), quanto (how much). Spoken Brazilian keeps statement word order (Você mora onde?) or fronts the question word, often with é que: Onde é que você mora?

drills.translation: Where is the metro station?
What is your name?
When does the film start?
Who is that young woman?
How do you say that in Portuguese?
How much does the ticket cost?
What are you doing?
Why didn't you come to the party?
drills.hint: why — asking for a reason (two words at the start of a question)

points.title: Adjective agreement
points.function: Match adjectives to their noun
points.explanation:
Adjectives agree in gender and number and usually FOLLOW the noun: um carro novo, uma casa nova, carros novos, casas novas.

Adjectives in -e or a consonant have one form for both genders (um filme interessante, uma aula interessante).

drills.translation: The food here is very good.
The apartment is new and bright.
The streets downtown are busy.
The tickets are expensive this year.
She is an intelligent person.
Portuguese is a beautiful language.

points.title: The past — pretérito perfeito (falei, comeu)
points.function: Say what happened (completed actions)
points.explanation:
The perfeito reports one completed event: falei 'I spoke', comeu 'he ate'.

-ar: falei, falou, falamos, falaram.

-er: comi, comeu, comemos, comeram.

-ir: abri, abriu, abrimos, abriram.

Time cues like ontem (yesterday), na semana passada, anteontem call for the perfeito. você shares the ele form (você falou).

drills.sentence: Ontem eu {{answer}} com a minha mãe.
drills.translation: Yesterday I spoke with my mother.
The day before yesterday I watched a great film.
She woke up late on Saturday.

drills.sentence: Você {{answer}} o recado ontem à noite?
drills.translation: Did you get the message last night?

drills.sentence: Na semana passada nós {{answer}} para a praia.
drills.translation: Last week we travelled to the beach.
We ate feijoada on Sunday.
They sold the house in December.

drills.sentence: As meninas {{answer}} cedo da festa ontem.
drills.translation: The girls left the party early yesterday.

points.title: The imperfect (falava, comia)
points.function: Describe how things used to be
points.explanation:
The imperfeito paints background and habit: quando era criança, eu morava no interior 'when I was a child, I lived in the countryside'.

-ar → -ava (falava, falávamos); -er/-ir → -ia (comia, abríamos).

Irregulars: ser (era), ter (tinha), vir (vinha), pôr (punha).

Cues: antigamente, sempre, todo dia, quando era criança.

drills.sentence: Antigamente eu {{answer}} de bicicleta para a escola.
drills.translation: I used to go to school by bike.

drills.sentence: Quando era criança, eu {{answer}} no quintal.
drills.translation: When I was a child, I used to play in the backyard.
My grandmother used to make cake every Sunday.
We used to live in a small house.
Back then, everything was cheaper.
They always used to play football in the street.

points.title: The future with ir (vou falar)
points.function: Say what is going to happen
points.explanation:
Brazil's everyday future is ir (present) + infinitive: vou viajar amanhã 'I'm going to travel tomorrow', vamos jantar fora 'we're going to eat out'. vou, vai, vamos, vão.

The one-word future (falarei) is formal/written — you'll read it, but conversation uses ir.

drills.translation: Tomorrow I'm going to wake up early.

drills.sentence: Eu {{answer}} ligar para você à noite.
drills.translation: I'm going to call you tonight.
She's going to arrive after lunch.
The game is going to start at eight.

drills.sentence: No sábado nós {{answer}} fazer um churrasco.
drills.translation: On Saturday we're going to have a barbecue.
We're going to visit you on vacation.

drills.sentence: Eles {{answer}} morar em Curitiba ano que vem.
drills.translation: They're going to live in Curitiba next year.

drills.sentence: As lojas {{answer}} abrir mais tarde amanhã.
drills.translation: The shops are going to open later tomorrow.

points.title: Contractions (no, da, dele, pelo…)
points.function: Merge prepositions with articles and pronouns
points.explanation:
Portuguese fuses prepositions with what follows: em + o = no, em + a = na, de + o = do, de + a = da, a + a = à, por + o = pelo, de + ele = dele, em + este = neste.

Moro no Rio, venho da Bahia, gosto dele.

drills.translation: I live in Brazil.
She works at the bakery on the corner.
This gift came from my aunt.
The bus goes through downtown.

drills.sentence: Gosto muito {{answer}} — ele é gente boa.
drills.translation: I really like him — he's a good person.
We just got back from the supermarket.

points.title: Object pronouns (me, te, o, a, nos)
points.function: Replace the object with a short pronoun
points.explanation:
me, te, o/a (him/her/it), nos, os/as. Brazilian Portuguese places them BEFORE the verb (ela me viu 'she saw me') and, in speech, often uses ele/ela instead of o/a (eu vi ele).

Learn the standard forms — you'll need them in writing.

drills.translation: She saw me at the mall yesterday.
I love you.

drills.sentence: Conhece meu chefe? Eu {{answer}} respeito muito.
drills.translation: Do you know my boss? I respect him a lot.

drills.sentence: A Maria? Eu {{answer}} encontrei na feira.
drills.translation: Maria? I met her at the street market.
Our friends invited us to dinner.

drills.sentence: Esses livros? Eu {{answer}} li nas férias.
drills.translation: Those books? I read them on vacation.

points.title: Comparatives (mais… do que, o mais)
points.function: Compare things
points.explanation:
mais + adjective + (do) que: mais quente do que 'hotter than'; menos for 'less'; tão … quanto 'as … as'. Superlative adds the article: o mais alto 'the tallest'.

Irregulars: bom → melhor, ruim → pior, grande → maior, pequeno → menor.

drills.sentence: São Paulo é {{answer}} do que Curitiba.
drills.translation: São Paulo is bigger than Curitiba.

drills.sentence: O açaí daqui é {{answer}} do que o do mercado.
drills.translation: The açaí here is better than the market's.

drills.sentence: Hoje está {{answer}} quente do que ontem.
drills.translation: Today it's hotter than yesterday.

drills.sentence: Este é o {{answer}} restaurante do bairro.
drills.translation: This is the best restaurant in the neighbourhood.

drills.sentence: O trânsito está {{answer}} do que de costume.
drills.translation: The traffic is worse than usual.

drills.sentence: Ela é tão simpática {{answer}} a irmã.
drills.translation: She is as nice as her sister.

points.title: Reflexive verbs (me levanto, se chama)
points.function: Say the subject acts on itself
points.explanation:
me, te, se, nos, se before the verb (Brazilian order): eu me levanto cedo 'I get up early', ela se chama Ana 'her name is Ana', nós nos falamos todo dia 'we talk every day'.

Spoken Brazil drops some reflexives (eu levanto cedo) — both are heard.

drills.sentence: Eu {{answer}} levanto às seis da manhã.
drills.translation: I get up at six in the morning.
I forgot her birthday!
What's your name? (how do you call yourself)
He hurt himself in the game.
We met (each other) at university.
We talk to each other on the phone.
They got married last year.
The children had fun at the party.

points.title: Possessives (meu, sua, dele…)
points.function: Say whose something is — without ambiguity
points.explanation:
meu/minha, teu/tua, seu/sua, nosso/nossa agree with the THING owned: meu carro, minha casa.

Here's the Brazilian twist: seu/sua usually means YOUR (de você); for his/her, Brazil says dele/dela after the noun — o carro dele 'his car', a bolsa dela 'her bag'.

That kills the ambiguity of seu.

drills.translation: My phone is ringing.
Is your house near the metro?
His car is at the garage.
Her mother is a teacher.
Our children study at the same school.
Their keys were left on the table.

points.title: Demonstratives (este, esse, aquele)
points.function: Point at things near me, near you, and far away
points.explanation:
Three distances: este/esta 'this (near me)', esse/essa 'that (near you)', aquele/aquela 'that over there'.

Neuters isto/isso/aquilo for unnamed things. In spoken Brazil este and esse blur together — but aquele is always the far one.

They also contract: neste, nesse, naquele; disto, disso, daquilo.

drills.translation: This phone here is mine.
Is that book (near you) good?
That building over there is the theatre.

drills.sentence: O que é {{answer}} na sua mão?
drills.translation: What is that in your hand?
This shop here sells everything.
Do you remember that trip we took?

points.title: Right now: estar + -ndo (estou falando)
points.function: Say what is happening at this moment
points.explanation:
Brazil's progressive is estar + gerúndio: estou falando 'I'm speaking', ela está trabalhando 'she's working'.

Gerúndio: -ar → -ando, -er → -endo, -ir → -indo.

Cues: agora, neste momento, ainda.
points.culture_note: European Portuguese says estar a + infinitive instead: estou a falar. Both mean exactly the same; the app teaches the Brazilian form and flags the European one here.

drills.translation: Hold on, I'm talking on the phone.
She's working right now, call later.
We're watching the game.
The children are sleeping in the bedroom.
It's raining hard outside.
I'm still writing the report.

points.title: Presente do subjuntivo (que eu fale)
points.function: Express wish, doubt, and emotion
points.explanation:
After wishes, doubts, and feelings — quero que, duvido que, espero que, é importante que, talvez — the verb goes to the subjunctive.

Build it from the eu-present: falo → fale, como → coma, abro → abra.

Key irregulars: ser (seja), estar (esteja), ir (vá), saber (saiba), haver (haja).

drills.translation: I want you to be at the party.
I hope everything is OK.
I doubt he knows the truth.
It's important that we arrive early.
Maybe they'll come tomorrow.
Let's hope it doesn't rain on the holiday.

points.title: The imperative (fala! / fale!)
points.function: Give instructions and advice
points.explanation:
The standard imperative for você takes the subjunctive form: fale mais devagar 'speak more slowly', abra a janela. Spoken Brazil freely mixes in the tu form without -s (fala!, abre!, vem cá!) — both are heard everywhere.

Negative commands always use the subjunctive: não fale.

drills.translation: Speak more slowly, please.
Open the door, it's hot.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} cá, quero te mostrar uma coisa!
drills.translation: Come here, I want to show you something!
Hold on, I'm almost there!
Don't tell that to anyone.

drills.sentence: Não se {{answer}}, vai dar tudo certo.
drills.translation: Don't worry, it'll all work out.

points.title: Tenho falado — the iterative perfect
points.function: Say what you've been doing lately
points.explanation:
A famous trap: ter + participle (tenho falado) is NOT English 'I have spoken'. It means repetition or continuity up to now — 'I've been speaking (lately)'.

Tenho trabalhado muito 'I've been working a lot (these days)'. For a single completed past event, Portuguese uses the simple perfeito: já falei com ele 'I've (already) spoken with him'.

drills.translation: Lately I've been working too much.
She's been studying every night.
We've been talking a lot this week.
They've been travelling a lot this year.
Have you been sleeping well?
It's been very cold lately.

points.title: Relative pronouns (que, quem, onde, cujo)
points.function: Join two ideas into one sentence
points.explanation: que is the all-purpose relative: o filme que eu vi 'the film I saw'. After a preposition use quem for people (a moça com quem falei) and que/o qual for things. onde = 'where'. cujo/cuja 'whose' agrees with the thing possessed and is formal — speech usually rephrases it.

drills.sentence: O bolo {{answer}} você fez estava ótimo.
drills.translation: The cake you made was great.

drills.sentence: A cidade {{answer}} eu nasci fica no interior.
drills.translation: The town where I was born is in the countryside.

drills.sentence: O amigo com {{answer}} eu moro é do Ceará.
drills.translation: The friend I live with is from Ceará.

drills.sentence: As pessoas {{answer}} chegaram cedo pegaram lugar.
drills.translation: The people who arrived early got seats.

drills.sentence: O autor {{answer}} livro ganhou o prêmio é baiano.
drills.translation: The author whose book won the prize is from Bahia.

drills.sentence: Não gostei do jeito {{answer}} ele falou comigo.
drills.translation: I didn't like the way he spoke to me.

points.title: The conditional (falaria, gostaria)
points.function: Be polite and talk about hypotheticals
points.explanation:
Add -ia to the infinitive: falaria 'would speak', comeria, abriria.

Irregulars: fazer → faria, dizer → diria, trazer → traria. gostaria de 'I would like' is the polite request; spoken Brazil often uses the imperfeito instead (eu queria um café = eu gostaria de um café).

drills.sentence: Eu {{answer}} de um suco de maracujá.
drills.translation: I would like a passion-fruit juice.

drills.sentence: Você {{answer}} me ajudar com a mudança?
drills.translation: Could you help me with the move?

drills.sentence: Nós {{answer}} mais tempo com as crianças.
drills.translation: We would spend more time with the kids.

drills.sentence: Ele disse que {{answer}} o trabalho hoje.
drills.translation: He said he would do the work today.

drills.sentence: No seu lugar, eu {{answer}} a proposta.
drills.translation: In your place, I would accept the offer.
They would love to live near the sea.

points.title: Pronoun placement (me diga / diga-me)
points.function: Put the pronoun where Brazilians put it
points.explanation:
Brazilian Portuguese prefers the pronoun BEFORE the verb (próclise): ele me viu, eu te ligo. After the verb (ênclise: diga-me) is formal writing and sentence-initial commands in careful style.

One firm rule everywhere: certain words attract the pronoun forward — não, que, quem, já, sempre, talvez: não ME diga, quero que SE sente.

drills.translation: She called me last night.

drills.sentence: Não {{answer}} esqueça de trazer o documento.
drills.translation: Don't forget to bring the document.
I'll let you know when I arrive.

drills.sentence: Quero que você {{answer}} sinta em casa.
drills.translation: I want you to feel at home.
I always remember that summer.
Have a seat, please. (formal)

points.title: Reported speech (disse que, perguntou se)
points.function: Report what someone said or asked
points.explanation:
Statements: disse que + shifted tense (presente → imperfeito, perfeito → mais-que-perfeito composto, futuro → condicional): 'vou sair' → disse que ia sair.

Yes/no questions: perguntou se. Requests: pediu que + subjuntivo (pediu que eu esperasse).

drills.translation: He said (that) he was tired.
She asked whether I wanted a ride.

drills.sentence: Ele falou que {{answer}} chegar mais tarde.
drills.translation: He said he was going to arrive later.

drills.sentence: A médica disse que eu {{answer}} descansar.
drills.translation: The doctor said I should rest.

drills.sentence: Meu chefe pediu que eu {{answer}} o relatório.
drills.translation: My boss asked me to finish the report.

drills.sentence: Avisaram que a reunião {{answer}} ser adiada.
drills.translation: They warned the meeting might be postponed.

points.title: Futuro do subjuntivo (quando eu falar, se eu quiser)
points.function: Talk about future conditions — Portuguese's signature tense
points.explanation:
Portuguese alone among the Romance languages has a FUTURE subjunctive, and it's everywhere: after quando, se, enquanto, assim que, depois que pointing at the future.

Build it from the eles-perfeito minus -am: falaram → falar, quiseram → quiser, fizeram → fizer, forem (ir/ser).

Quando eu CHEGAR, te ligo 'when I arrive, I'll call you'.

drills.sentence: Quando eu {{answer}} em casa, te ligo.
drills.translation: When I get home, I'll call you.

drills.sentence: Se você {{answer}}, a gente vai junto.
drills.translation: If you want, we'll go together.

drills.sentence: Assim que ela {{answer}} o e-mail, me avisa.
drills.translation: As soon as she sends the email, let me know.
As long as we're here, it's fine.

drills.sentence: Se eles {{answer}} tempo, passam aqui depois.
drills.translation: If they have time, they'll stop by later.

drills.sentence: Quando vocês {{answer}} ao Brasil, me visitem!
drills.translation: When you come to Brazil, visit me!
drills.hint: come — you all (future subjunctive of vir)

points.title: Imperfeito do subjuntivo (se eu falasse)
points.function: Imagine unreal situations
points.explanation:
For unreal or hypothetical conditions: se + imperfeito do subjuntivo pairs with the conditional (or, in speech, the imperfeito): se eu TIVESSE dinheiro, viajaria/viajava 'if I had money, I'd travel'.

Build from the eles-perfeito minus -ram + -sse: falaram → falasse, tiveram → tivesse, fossem (ser/ir).

drills.sentence: Se eu {{answer}} rico, morava na praia.
drills.translation: If I were rich, I'd live at the beach.

drills.sentence: Se ela {{answer}} mais cedo, pegava o voo.
drills.translation: If she left earlier, she'd catch the flight.

drills.sentence: Se nós {{answer}} um carro, seria mais fácil.
drills.translation: If we had a car, it would be easier.
I wish you were here.

drills.sentence: Se eles {{answer}} a verdade, ficariam bravos.
drills.translation: If they knew the truth, they'd be angry.
It was as if time stopped.

points.title: Infinitivo pessoal (para fazermos)
points.function: Conjugate the infinitive — another Portuguese exclusive
points.explanation:
The personal infinitive gives the infinitive endings so each doer is clear: para eu fazer, para você fazer, para nós fazermos, para eles fazerem.

É melhor VOCÊS irem agora 'it's better for you all to go now'. Used after para, antes de, depois de, sem, até and impersonal expressions.

Unique to Portuguese (and Galician).

drills.sentence: Trouxe o mapa para nós {{answer}} o caminho.
drills.translation: I brought the map so we could find the way.

drills.sentence: É melhor vocês {{answer}} antes do trânsito.
drills.translation: It's better for you all to leave before the traffic.

drills.sentence: Antes de eles {{answer}}, vamos arrumar a casa.
drills.translation: Before they arrive, let's tidy the house.

drills.sentence: Depois de nós {{answer}}, conversamos com calma.
drills.translation: After we have dinner, we'll talk calmly.
She left without her children noticing.

drills.sentence: O ideal é vocês {{answer}} cedo para o show.
drills.translation: The ideal is for you all to arrive early for the show.

points.title: The passive (foi construído; vende-se)
points.function: Say something is done without naming the doer
points.explanation: Formal passive: ser + participle agreeing with the subject — a casa foi construída 'the house was built'; the agent takes por. Everyday Portuguese prefers the se-passive: vende-se casa 'house for sale', fala-se português aqui — or just an impersonal eles/a gente.

drills.translation: The museum was opened in 1950.

drills.sentence: As ruas {{answer}} fechadas para o desfile.
drills.translation: The streets were closed for the parade.
Apartment for rent downtown.
A lot of fresh fish is sold here.

drills.sentence: O quadro foi pintado {{answer}} uma artista mineira.
drills.translation: The painting was painted by an artist from Minas.
The decision will be announced tomorrow.

points.title: Ficar — become, stay, be located, keep on
points.function: Master Portuguese's most versatile verb
points.explanation: ficar covers what several English verbs do: location (o hotel fica na praia 'the hotel is on the beach'), staying (fiquei em casa), becoming (fiquei feliz 'I got happy'), and ficar + gerúndio 'to keep doing' (fica reclamando 'keeps complaining'). ficar com = to keep/take.

drills.sentence: A farmácia {{answer}} ao lado do banco.
drills.translation: The pharmacy is next to the bank.
Yesterday I stayed home resting.

drills.sentence: Ela {{answer}} muito feliz com a notícia.
drills.translation: She got very happy with the news.

drills.sentence: Não {{answer}} bravo comigo, foi sem querer.
drills.translation: Don't get mad at me, it was an accident.
He keeps complaining all day long.
You can keep the change.

points.title: Connectors (porém, portanto, embora)
points.function: Link ideas: contrast, consequence, concession
points.explanation: porém/no entanto 'however', portanto/por isso 'therefore', além disso 'besides', embora + SUBJUNTIVO 'although' (embora seja caro 'although it's expensive'), mesmo que + subjuntivo 'even if', já que 'since'.

drills.sentence: Está chovendo, {{answer}} isso vamos de carro.
drills.translation: It's raining, so we'll go by car.

drills.sentence: O hotel é caro; {{answer}}, vale a pena.
drills.translation: The hotel is expensive; however, it's worth it.
Although it's late, there's still time.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} que chova, o show vai acontecer.
drills.translation: Even if it rains, the show will go on.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} que você está aqui, me ajuda?
drills.translation: Since you're here, can you help me?
He studied hard; therefore, he passed easily.

points.title: Compound subjunctives (tenha falado, tivesse ido)
points.function: Handle completed actions inside subjunctive contexts
points.explanation:
Perfect subjunctive: tenha + participle for a completed action after a present trigger — espero que tenha dado tudo certo 'I hope it all went well'.

Pluperfect subjunctive: tivesse + participle for past unreals — se eu tivesse sabido, teria vindo 'if I had known, I would have come' (result: teria + participle).

drills.sentence: Espero que você {{answer}} gostado do presente.
drills.translation: I hope you liked the gift.

drills.sentence: Duvido que eles {{answer}} chegado a tempo.
drills.translation: I doubt they arrived in time.
If I had known, I would have warned you.

drills.sentence: Se nós {{answer}} saído antes, não perdíamos o voo.
drills.translation: If we had left earlier, we wouldn't have missed the flight.

drills.sentence: Ela {{answer}} vindo, mas o carro quebrou.
drills.translation: She would have come, but the car broke down.

drills.sentence: O que você {{answer}} feito no meu lugar?
drills.translation: What would you have done in my place?

points.title: Crase and regência (vou à praia)
points.function: Get à right — and the prepositions verbs demand
points.explanation:
Crase (à) marks a + a colliding: vou à praia (a + a praia) but vou a São Paulo (no article). Never before verbs, masculine nouns, or most pronouns.

Regência is which preposition a verb demands: assistir A um filme, gostar DE, precisar DE, depender DE, chegar A — spoken Brazil bends some of these, but writing expects them.

drills.sentence: Amanhã vou {{answer}} praia com as crianças.
drills.translation: Tomorrow I'm going to the beach with the kids.

drills.sentence: Chegamos {{answer}} conclusão de que valia a pena.
drills.translation: We reached the conclusion that it was worth it.
Did you watch the interview yesterday?
I need your help on Saturday.
That depends on the weather on Friday.
She replied to all the messages.
drills.hint: to (responder a — no article, no crase)

points.title: Formal pronoun placement (mesóclise and ênclise)
points.function: Read and produce the formal written norms
points.explanation:
Formal writing starts no sentence with a pronoun: Diga-me (ênclise), never Me diga. In the future and conditional, the formal norm is mesóclise — the pronoun INSIDE the verb: dir-te-ei 'I shall tell you', far-se-á 'it shall be done'.

You'll meet it in literature, law, and speeches; nobody says it on the street, but educated readers expect you to parse it.

drills.translation: Tell me the truth, please. (formal)
Hand me the document tomorrow. (formal)
I shall tell you when the time comes.
Justice shall be done.
They invited us to the ceremony. (formal)
In case of emergency, call 192.

points.title: Focus & clefts (foi ele que…, é que)
points.function: Foreground exactly the word you mean
points.explanation:
Portuguese clefts with ser + que: foi ELE que quebrou o copo 'HE's the one who broke the glass', é HOJE que decide 'TODAY is when it's decided'. Question softener é que: onde é que você mora?

Spoken Brazil also doubles the subject for topic ('o João, ele trabalha aqui') and uses 'que nem' for 'just like'.

drills.sentence: Foi ele {{answer}} quebrou o copo, não eu.
drills.translation: It was he who broke the glass, not me.
It's tomorrow that the result comes out.

drills.sentence: Onde é {{answer}} você deixou as chaves?
drills.translation: Where (is it that) you left the keys?
I'm the one paying this time!

drills.sentence: Não foi aqui {{answer}} a gente se conheceu?
drills.translation: Wasn't it here that we met?

drills.sentence: O que é {{answer}} está acontecendo ali?
drills.translation: What (is it that) is going on over there?

points.title: Register: Brazil, Portugal, and the written norm
points.function: Move between spoken Brazilian, European, and formal writing
points.explanation:
Three registers to command: spoken Brazilian (você + proclitic pronouns, estar + -ndo, 'a gente' for we), European Portuguese (tu informal, estar a + infinitive, ênclise: dá-me), and the shared formal written norm (full relative cujo, mesóclise, connectors like todavia, não obstante, outrossim in legal prose).

The educated reader switches without blinking; know which room you're in.

drills.sentence: {{answer}} gente se vê no sábado! (spoken BR)
drills.translation: See you Saturday! (lit. the people see each other)
I'm working right now. (European progressive)

drills.sentence: O projeto é caro; {{answer}}, é necessário. (formal)
drills.translation: The project is expensive; nevertheless, it is necessary.
Notwithstanding that, he decided to proceed.

drills.sentence: Ele fala {{answer}} nem o pai. (spoken BR)
drills.translation: He talks just like his father.
I ask that you send me the contract.

points.title: Key irregular presents (vou, faço, posso, quero)
points.function: Use the everyday irregular verbs
points.explanation:
A handful of essential verbs bend the present tense:

ir (to go): vou, vai, vamos, vão. fazer (to do/make): faço, faz, fazemos, fazem. poder (can): posso, pode, podemos, podem. querer (to want): quero, quer, queremos, querem.

Also worth knowing on sight: ver (vejo, vê), vir (venho, vem), dizer (digo, diz), saber (sei, sabe). Usually only the eu form (and sometimes the stem) is irregular — the endings stay familiar.

related.title: The future with ir (vou falar)
related.contrast: The irregular vou/vai/vamos is the engine of the everyday future.

drills.translation: I'm going to the market now.
I exercise every morning.
Can you help me with this?
We want to travel in December.
I can't see anything from here.
They're coming to our house tomorrow.

points.title: Pretérito perfeito — irregulares (fui, fiz, tive)
points.function: Tell what happened — the irregular pasts
points.explanation:
The most common verbs have irregular pasts you simply learn:

ser AND ir share one past: fui, foi, fomos, foram (context decides: Fui médico / Fui ao Rio). fazer: fiz, fez, fizemos, fizeram. ter: tive, teve, tivemos, tiveram. estar: estive, esteve… poder: pude, pôde… querer: quis, quis… dizer: disse, disse…

No -ei/-ou endings here — these stems are their own little words.

related.title: The past — pretérito perfeito (falei, comeu)
related.contrast: Regular verbs take -ei/-ou; these dozen verbs change stem entirely.

drills.translation: Yesterday I went to the cinema.
She made a delicious cake.
We had a long meeting today.

drills.sentence: Você {{answer}} no Brasil no ano passado?
drills.translation: Were you in Brazil last year?
They couldn't come to the party.

drills.sentence: O que ele {{answer}} sobre o projeto?
drills.translation: What did he say about the project?
